_comment: Seed knowledge graph for the KG-RAG Legal Assistant. Real Indian statutes and landmark Supreme Court cases. Texts are paraphrased summaries for demo purposes, NOT verbatim statutory text.

acts.title: Indian Penal Code
acts.current_status: largely replaced by Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023

acts.title: Code of Criminal Procedure
acts.current_status: replaced by Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023

acts.title: Information Technology Act
acts.ministry: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology

sections.title: Punishment for murder
sections.text: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
sections.explanation: Murder is defined in Section 300. Punishment under 302 is death or life imprisonment.

sections.title: Murder
sections.text: Culpable homicide is murder if the act causing death is done with the intention of causing death, subject to exceptions such as grave and sudden provocation.
sections.proviso: Exception 1: grave and sudden provocation. Exception 4: sudden fight.
sections.explanation: Defines when culpable homicide amounts to murder.

sections.title: Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property
sections.text: Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property shall be punished with imprisonment up to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
sections.explanation: Punishment for cheating with dishonest inducement of property delivery.

sections.title: Acts done by several persons in furtherance of common intention
sections.text: When a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention of all, each person is liable as if the act were done by him alone.
sections.explanation: Establishes joint/constructive liability via common intention.

sections.title: Punishment for murder
sections.text: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life and shall also be liable to fine. Mob lynching on specified grounds is separately punishable.
sections.explanation: BNS successor provision to IPC Section 302.

sections.title: When bail may be taken in case of non-bailable offence
sections.text: A person accused of a non-bailable offence may be released on bail, but not if there appear reasonable grounds that he is guilty of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life.
sections.proviso: Proviso: the court may direct release of a person under sixteen, a woman, or a sick or infirm person.
sections.explanation: Bail in non-bailable offences before a Magistrate.

sections.title: Direction for grant of bail to person apprehending arrest (anticipatory bail)
sections.text: Where any person has reason to believe that he may be arrested on accusation of a non-bailable offence, he may apply to the High Court or Court of Session for a direction that he be released on bail in the event of arrest.
sections.explanation: Anticipatory bail; jurisdiction lies with Sessions Court and High Court.

sections.title: When police may arrest without warrant
sections.text: A police officer may arrest without a warrant a person concerned in a cognizable offence, subject to recording reasons where the offence is punishable with up to seven years.
sections.proviso: Amended in 2009 to require recorded reasons and a notice of appearance under Section 41A.
sections.explanation: Conditions for warrantless arrest; tightened post-2009.

sections.title: Protection of life and personal liberty
sections.text: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.
sections.explanation: Fundamental right; expanded by Maneka Gandhi to require fair, just and reasonable procedure.

sections.title: Equality before law
sections.text: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.

sections.title: Agreement in restraint of trade void
sections.text: Every agreement by which anyone is restrained from exercising a lawful profession, trade or business is to that extent void.
sections.explanation: Basis for the general unenforceability of post-employment non-compete clauses in India.

sections.title: Punishment for sending offensive messages (struck down)
sections.text: Provided punishment for sending offensive messages through communication services. Struck down as unconstitutional in 2015.
sections.explanation: Inserted by the 2008 amendment; held void in Shreya Singhal v Union of India.

cases.title: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab
cases.outcome: Death penalty constitutional; rarest of rare doctrine established
cases.summary: Upheld constitutional validity of the death penalty under Section 302 IPC and laid down the 'rarest of rare' doctrine limiting its imposition.

cases.title: Mithu v. State of Punjab
cases.summary: Struck down mandatory death penalty under Section 303 IPC as violative of Articles 14 and 21.

cases.title: Siddharam Satlingappa Mhetre v. State of Maharashtra
cases.summary: Laid down comprehensive guidelines for grant of anticipatory bail under Section 438 CrPC; anticipatory bail can ordinarily continue till end of trial.

cases.title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
cases.outcome: Article 21 procedure must be fair, just and reasonable
cases.summary: Expanded Article 21: 'procedure established by law' must be fair, just and reasonable, linking Articles 14, 19 and 21.

cases.title: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
cases.summary: Struck down Section 66A of the IT Act 2000 as an unconstitutional restriction on free speech under Article 19(1)(a).

cases.title: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
cases.summary: Directed police to follow Section 41 CrPC checklist before arrest in offences punishable up to seven years; curbs automatic arrest.

cases.title: State v. Ramesh (murder, premeditated)
cases.summary: Conviction under Section 302 upheld; premeditation and corroborated eyewitness evidence.

cases.title: State v. Suresh (sudden fight)
cases.summary: Converted from 302 to 304 Part II; grave and sudden provocation and absence of premeditation.

cases.title: State v. Mahesh (circumstantial)
cases.summary: Conviction sustained on a complete chain of circumstantial evidence.

cases.title: State v. Dinesh (weak evidence)
cases.summary: Acquittal due to hostile witnesses and absence of corroboration.

cases.title: X v. State (anticipatory bail, economic offence)
cases.summary: Anticipatory bail granted; accused cooperative and no custodial interrogation needed.

cases.title: Y v. State (anticipatory bail, heinous offence)
cases.summary: Anticipatory bail denied for a serious offence with risk of tampering.

cases.title: State v. Anil (cheating, large scale)
cases.summary: Conviction under Section 420; clear dishonest inducement and money trail.

cases.title: State v. Pooja (civil dispute)
cases.summary: Acquittal; transaction was essentially a civil dispute without initial dishonest intent.

concepts.name: murder
concepts.definition: Unlawful killing with the intention or knowledge defined under Section 300 IPC.

concepts.name: bail
concepts.definition: Release of an accused person from custody, often on conditions, pending trial.

concepts.name: anticipatory bail
concepts.definition: A direction to release a person on bail in the event of a future arrest.

concepts.name: non-compete
concepts.definition: A clause restraining a person from competing or working with a competitor, generally void post-employment in India under Section 27 Contract Act.

concepts.name: right to life
concepts.definition: Fundamental right under Article 21 to life and personal liberty.

amendments.old_text: Police could arrest in any cognizable offence with wide discretion.
amendments.new_text: For offences punishable up to seven years, officer must record reasons and may issue notice of appearance (Section 41A).
Inserted Section 66A penalising offensive electronic messages (later struck down in 2015).
